content.text: What Task Automation Means for Agents
Task automation is the use of an agent to carry out a multi-step workflow with
minimal human involvement

once the task is defined. Unlike a traditional script, an agent does not follow a fixed sequence of steps. It senses the current state, evaluates whether the goal is met, and selects the best next action — adapting when intermediate results are unexpected.

This adaptability is what distinguishes agent-based automation from simple scripting. A script breaks when the environment deviates from the expected sequence. An agent handles deviation by re-planning rather than failing.

attrs.alt: Comparison of fixed-script vs agent-based task automation
attrs.title: Task Automation: Script vs. Agent

content.text: Categories of Automatable Tasks

Agents are well-suited for tasks that share three characteristics: the outcome can be defined as a measurable goal state, the steps required vary depending on what is discovered along the way, and the task involves more steps than a human would want to perform manually each time.
Category
Example tasks
Why an agent helps
Code maintenance
Fix lint errors, update dependencies, write tests
Steps depend on what errors are found
Data processing
Extract, clean, transform, and load records
Schema mismatches require conditional handling
Research & synthesis
Search docs, summarise findings, draft report
What to search next depends on what was found
Infrastructure ops
Provision, configure, test, and clean up resources
Config choices depend on detected environment state
Customer workflows
Handle support tickets, update CRM, send follow-ups
Each ticket has a different resolution path
Building a Task Automation Agent

A task automation agent needs four things: a goal specification that defines when the task is done, a set of tools it can invoke, a loop that senses the current state and selects the next action, and a budget that prevents runaway execution.
LLM-Powered Task Automation

LLM-based agents replace the hand-coded action-selection logic with an LLM that reads the current state and decides which tool to call. This allows the same agent loop to handle a far wider range of tasks — the LLM figures out the steps rather than requiring them to be hard-coded.

import json

# Tool definitions that the LLM can select from
TOOLS = [
    {
        'name': 'run_linter',
        'description': 'Run the code linter and return a list of errors',
        'parameters': {},
    },
    {
        'name': 'fix_lint_error',
        'description': 'Fix a specific lint error in a file',
        'parameters': {'file': 'string', 'line': 'integer', 'rule': 'string'},
    },
    {
        'name': 'run_tests',
        'description': 'Run the test suite and return pass/fail status',
        'parameters': {},
    },
    {
        'name': 'mark_complete',
        'description': 'Signal that the task is complete',
        'parameters': {'summary': 'string'},
    },
]

SYSTEM_PROMPT = '''You are a code quality automation agent.

Goal: Fix all lint errors so that run_linter returns an empty list AND run_tests passes.

Tools available: run_linter, fix_lint_error, run_tests, mark_complete.

Always start by running the linter to see the current state.
Call mark_complete only when both conditions are true.'''

def llm_automation_loop(llm_call, tool_executor, max_steps=10):
    messages = [{'role': 'system', 'content': SYSTEM_PROMPT}]
    messages.append({'role': 'user', 'content': 'Start the code quality task now.'})

    for step in range(max_steps):
        response = llm_call(messages, tools=TOOLS)
        messages.append({'role': 'assistant', 'content': str(response)})

        tool_name = response.get('tool')
        tool_args = response.get('args', {})

        if not tool_name:
            print('No tool call -- agent may be confused')
            break

        if tool_name == 'mark_complete':
            print('Task complete:', tool_args.get('summary'))
            return True

        # Execute the chosen tool
        result = tool_executor(tool_name, tool_args)
        print('Step ' + str(step + 1) + ': ' + tool_name + ' -> ' + str(result)[:80])

        # Feed the result back to the LLM as context
        messages.append({'role': 'user', 'content': 'Tool result: ' + json.dumps(result)})

    print('Max steps reached')
    return False
When to Use an Agent vs. a Script
Agents add complexity. Use one only when that complexity is justified by the adaptability it provides.
Use a script
when the task always follows the same steps, errors are handled by aborting and retrying, and the environment is predictable and well-understood.
Use an agent

when the right sequence of actions depends on intermediate outcomes, when handling edge cases would require dozens of if/else branches in a script, or when the task involves open-ended decision making.
Never automate without a budget
: always set a maximum number of steps or a wall-clock timeout. A stuck agent consuming API credits indefinitely is worse than a failed script.
Safety Practices for Automated Tasks

Automated agents act without a human reviewing every step. This creates two risks: the agent takes an action with unintended side-effects, and the agent goes in circles consuming resources without making progress. Both risks need explicit mitigations.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable
import time

@dataclass
class ActionPolicy:
    name: str
    is_reversible: bool
    requires_approval: bool = False
    rate_limit_per_minute: int = 60

class SafeAutomationAgent:
    def __init__(self, policies, approve_fn=None):
        self.policies = {p.name: p for p in policies}
        self.approve_fn = approve_fn  # callable(action, args) -> bool
        self.action_counts = {}       # action_name -> (window_start, count)

    def execute(self, action_name, args, action_fn):
        policy = self.policies.get(action_name)
        if policy is None:
            raise ValueError('Unknown action: ' + action_name)

        # Rate limit check
        self._check_rate_limit(policy)

        # Approval gate for irreversible actions
        if not policy.is_reversible and policy.requires_approval:
            if self.approve_fn is None:
                raise RuntimeError('Irreversible action requires approval but no approve_fn set')
            approved = self.approve_fn(action_name, args)
            if not approved:
                print('Action ' + action_name + ' rejected by approver')
                return None

        result = action_fn(args)
        self._record_action(policy)
        return result

    def _check_rate_limit(self, policy):
        now = time.time()
        key = policy.name
        if key not in self.action_counts:
            self.action_counts[key] = (now, 0)
        window_start, count = self.action_counts[key]
        if now - window_start > 60:
            self.action_counts[key] = (now, 0)
            window_start, count = now, 0
        if count >= policy.rate_limit_per_minute:
            raise RuntimeError('Rate limit reached for: ' + policy.name)

    def _record_action(self, policy):
        key = policy.name
        window_start, count = self.action_counts.get(key, (time.time(), 0))
        self.action_counts[key] = (window_start, count + 1)

# Policy definitions
policies = [
    ActionPolicy('read_file', is_reversible=True, rate_limit_per_minute=120),
    ActionPolicy('write_file', is_reversible=False, requires_approval=False, rate_limit_per_minute=20),
    ActionPolicy('delete_file', is_reversible=False, requires_approval=True, rate_limit_per_minute=5),
    ActionPolicy('run_tests', is_reversible=True, rate_limit_per_minute=10),
    ActionPolicy('deploy', is_reversible=False, requires_approval=True, rate_limit_per_minute=2),
]

def auto_approve(action, args):
    # In production: send notification, wait for human response
    print('Auto-approving ' + action + ' (demo mode)')
    return True

agent = SafeAutomationAgent(policies, approve_fn=auto_approve)
Observability: Knowing What the Agent Did

An automated agent that produces no logs is a black box. When it fails or produces an unexpected result, there is no way to diagnose the problem. Build structured logging into every task automation agent from the start.
Idempotency: Running Automation Safely More Than Once
An automated task may be interrupted and restarted. If the agent is not
idempotent
— able to run multiple times without producing duplicate or conflicting results — partial failures can leave the system in a broken state. Design each action so that running it a second time is safe.
Scheduling and Triggering Automated Tasks
Task automation agents can be triggered in three ways. The right trigger depends on whether the task should run on a schedule, in response to an event, or on demand.
Trigger type
Example
Best for
Scheduled (cron)
Nightly dependency audit, weekly report generation
Periodic maintenance tasks with no external trigger
Event-driven
PR opened, file uploaded, alert fired
Tasks that must respond promptly to an external change
On-demand
User submits a task via chat, CLI, or API call
Variable or one-off tasks that differ each run
Testing Task Automation Agents
Testing an agent is harder than testing a function because the agent's output depends on the sequence of actions taken across multiple steps. Two approaches work well:
unit-testing each tool
in isolation, and
integration-testing the full loop
against a simulated environment.
Summary
Task automation agents replace fixed scripts with adaptive sense-decide-act loops that handle unexpected intermediate states.
Good automation targets: tasks with variable step sequences, conditional branching based on discovered state, and multi-step workflows that are tedious to do manually each time.
LLM agents extend automation to open-ended tasks by using the model's reasoning to select the next tool call rather than hard-coding the decision logic.
Always impose step and time budgets, classify actions by reversibility, and require approval for irreversible actions.
Log every action with timing and outcome to make the agent's behaviour auditable and debuggable.
